tech.headline: Fraunhofer IWU Storage Analyzer Cuts Factory Peak Loads
tech.summary: Fraunhofer IWU developed an energy storage systems in production (ESiP) analyzer that enables factories to use nearly half of their on-site generated electricity. The technology helps reduce peak loads by optimizing energy storage and usage in manufacturing environments.

tech.headline: JinkoSolar Achieves 34.82% Perovskite-Silicon Tandem Efficiency
tech.summary: JinkoSolar reached 34.82% efficiency for a perovskite-silicon tandem solar cell, validated by China's Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology. This result highlights progress in tandem cell technology for higher solar energy conversion rates.

tech.headline: Battery-Free Artificial Photosynthesis for Solar Fuel
tech.summary: Osaka Metropolitan University scientists developed a battery-free artificial photosynthesis system. The system self-regulates energy output without external electronics or batteries. It uses a solid electrolyte to adapt to sunlight fluctuations. This innovation promises cheaper and cleaner solar fuel production. The technology eliminates reliance on external power sources for energy management. The breakthrough could revolutionize sustainable energy generation and storage methods. The research highlights advancements in autonomous energy systems and renewable resource utilization.

tech.headline: Wallbox Launches Pulsar Pro to Simplify EV Charging Reimbursement
tech.summary: Wallbox has announced the launch of the new Pulsar Pro, the latest evolution of its AC charging portfolio. This solution is designed to simplify EV charging reimbursement as electric vehicle adoption continues to grow and charging becomes increasingly distributed across homes. The company is a leading provider of energy management solutions and electric vehicle charging.
tech.searchQuery: Wallbox Launches New Pulsar Pro to Simplify EV Cha 2026

learn.title: Power Purchase Agreements Explained
learn.intro: PPAs are contracts that let companies buy renewable energy directly from developers, cutting costs and accelerating the shift to clean power. They’re key to scaling solar and wind projects globally.
learn.c1_text: A Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) is a long-term contract where a buyer (like a corporation) agrees to purchase electricity from a renewable energy project at a fixed price. For example, a company might lock in electricity from a 100 MW solar farm for 15 years.
learn.c2_text: PPAs reduce financing risks for developers by securing revenue upfront. In 2023, PPAs covered 40% of global renewable energy deals, enabling projects to secure loans and bypass traditional utility barriers.
learn.c3_text: India’s renewable energy sector has seen a surge in PPAs, with 13 GW of solar and wind projects signed in 2023. The government’s renewable purchase obligations (RPOs) mandate utilities to source 40% of power from renewables by 2030, driving demand.
learn.stat1: India: 13 GW solar/wind PPAs signed in 2023
learn.stat3: India’s RPO target: 50% renewable energy by 2030
learn.stat4: India: 40% of new renewable projects use PPAs

learn.title: The Duck Curve Challenge
learn.intro: Solar’s rapid rise causes a 'duck curve' on the grid—excess midday energy and evening shortages. Managing this is critical for grid stability as renewables grow.
learn.c1_text: The 'duck curve' describes how solar energy’s midday surplus (peak around 2 PM) creates a steep drop in demand, followed by a sharp evening spike as solar fades. This strains grids reliant on fossil fuels for balancing.
learn.c2_text: Without storage or flexible resources, the duck curve forces utilities to ramp up gas plants rapidly at sunset, increasing costs and emissions. California’s grid faces this daily, with solar providing 25% of peak summer demand.
learn.c3_text: India’s grid struggles with similar issues as solar adoption grows. In 2023, solar met 10% of peak demand in Rajasthan, but evening shortages require coal plants to ramp up, undermining emissions goals.
learn.stat1: India: Solar met 10% peak demand in Rajasthan (2023)
learn.stat2: California: Solar covers 25% of peak demand
learn.stat3: India: Coal plants ramped 15% faster in 2023
learn.stat4: India: 18% of grid capacity is solar (2024)

learn.title: Hydrogen’s Color Code
learn.intro: Hydrogen’s color codes (green, blue, grey) reveal its production method and climate impact. Understanding these distinctions is vital for a sustainable hydrogen economy.
learn.c1_text: Grey hydrogen (95% of global production) is made from fossil fuels with CO₂ emissions. Green hydrogen uses renewable-powered electrolysis, while blue adds carbon capture. India aims for 5 million tonnes of green hydrogen by 2030.
learn.c2_text: Grey hydrogen emits 12 kg CO₂ per kg, while green emits near-zero. Blue hydrogen reduces emissions by 90% but requires costly carbon capture. India’s National Hydrogen Mission targets 100% renewable-powered production by 2030.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2023 National Hydrogen Mission plans 125 GW of renewable capacity to support green hydrogen. Pilot projects in Gujarat and Tamil Nadu aim to produce 1 million tonnes annually by 2025.
learn.stat1: India: 5 million tonnes green hydrogen target by 2030
learn.stat3: India: 125 GW renewable for hydrogen by 2030
learn.stat4: India: 10% of global green hydrogen plans (2023)

learn.title: Iron-Air Batteries for Storage
learn.intro: Iron-air batteries store energy for 100+ hours using abundant materials, offering a low-cost solution for long-duration storage to balance renewable energy grids.
learn.c1_text: Iron-air batteries store energy by converting iron into rust (iron oxide) during charging. Discharging reverses the process. They’re cheaper than lithium-ion and use 80% less land.
learn.c2_text: With 10x the duration of lithium-ion, iron-air batteries can store energy for weeks. A 2023 study showed they cost $20/kWh vs. $150/kWh for lithium-ion, making them ideal for grid-scale storage.
learn.c3_text: India’s 2023 National Energy Storage Mission targets 15 GW of storage by 2025. Iron-air tech could support solar-rich regions like Gujarat, where 24/7 power is critical.

learn.title: How EVs Work
learn.intro: Electric vehicles use motors, regenerative braking, and charging systems to convert electricity into motion. They’re pivotal for reducing transport emissions.
learn.c1_text: EVs use electric motors powered by batteries. Regenerative braking recaptures energy during deceleration, boosting efficiency. A Tesla Model 3 uses 15 kWh/100 miles, cutting emissions by 50% vs. gasoline cars.
learn.c2_text: Level 1 (120V) charges slowly (2-4 miles/hour), while Level 2 (240V) adds 25 miles/hour. DC fast chargers (480V) reach 80% in 30 minutes. India’s FAME scheme subsidizes 40% of EV charging infrastructure costs.
learn.c3_text: India aims for 30% EV sales by 2030. In 2023, 1.5 million EVs were sold, with Tamil Nadu leading in charging stations (1,200+). The government plans 200 GW of renewable energy to power EVs by 2030.
learn.stat4: India: 200 GW renewable for EVs by 2030

learn.title: LCOE: Comparing Energy Costs
learn.intro: Levelised Cost of Energy (LCOE) measures the average cost of electricity over a project’s lifetime, helping compare solar, wind, and fossil fuels. It’s essential for policy and investment decisions.
learn.c1_text: LCOE calculates the cost per kWh by dividing total project costs (construction, operation, financing) by total energy produced. For example, a solar farm with $1 billion cost and 100 GW-years output has an LCOE of $10/MWh.
learn.c2_text: Solar LCOE fell 82% since 2010, now $0.048/kWh (2023). Coal’s LCOE is $0.065/kWh, but without carbon pricing, it remains competitive. India’s solar LCOE is $0.035/kWh, making it cheaper than coal.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar LCOE dropped 60% since 2017, reaching $0.035/kWh in 2023. The government’s 2022 solar auction set a record low of $0.029/kWh. Wind LCOE is $0.042/kWh, driving rapid adoption.
